items.title: typical age
items.contentHtml:
Typical age: 10-16 years

  • Femoral epiphysiolysis classically occurs between 10 and 16 years, coinciding with the pubertal growth spurt, when the proximal physis of the femur is more vulnerable due to the increase in shear forces.
  • In males it is more frequent (2-4:1) and usually occurs at slightly older ages. early (12-14 years) than in women (11-13 years).
  • Atypical presentation outside this age range requires ruling out secondary causes such as endocrinopathies (hypothyroidism, growth hormone deficiency, hypogonadism) or metabolic disorders.
  • During the examination, asking about the exact age and Tanner stage helps confirm clinical suspicion.

items.foco: Obesity as a main risk factor
items.title: Obesity as a risk factor
items.contentHtml:
Obesity as a major risk factor

  • High body mass index (BMI) is the most important risk factor, as excessive mechanical loading on the proximal physis weakens the growth plate.
  • Obesity (BMI > 95th percentile for age and sex) is associated with up to a 10-fold increase in the risk of developing femoral epiphysiolysis.
  • The physis becomes wider and disorganized by chronic pressure, which predisposes it to displacement.
  • In the obese patient, the presentation can be insidious and pain referred to the knee is more frequent, which delays the diagnosis.

items.foco: Clinical presentation: lameness and pain in the hip or knee
items.title: Clinical presentation
items.contentHtml:
Clinical presentation: limping and pain in the hip or knee

  • The cardinal symptom is antalgic lameness (Trendelenburg gait) accompanied by pain in the hip or referred to the anterior region of the thigh or knee (due to irritation of the obturator nerve).
  • Pain referred to the knee without local pathology is a classic trap: up to 25% of patients initially consult for pain in the knee. knee.
  • Restriction of internal rotation of the hip is the earliest finding in the physical examination.
  • In advanced stages, the affected limb adopts an attitude of external rotation and relative shortening.

items.title: Drehmann's sign
items.contentHtml:
Positive Drehmann sign on examination

  • The Drehmann sign is considered positive when, when passively flexing the hip, the thigh involuntarily deviates in external rotation and abduction.
  • This sign is pathognomonic of femoral epiphysiolysis and reflects the posterior displacement of the epiphysis on the femoral neck.
  • It should be evaluated in supine position, flexing the hip at 90°; automatic external rotation indicates instability or significant displacement.
  • A positive Drehmann sign has a very high specificity and requires immediate radiographs.

items.title: Loder Classification
items.contentHtml:
Loder Classification (stable vs. unstable)

  • The Loder Classification divides femoral epiphysiolysis into stable (the patient can bear weight, with or without crutches) and unstable (inability to bear weight, even with assistance).
  • The unstable form is associated with a much higher risk of avascular necrosis of the head. femoral (up to 50%) and requires urgent surgical fixation.
  • Clinical stability is more important than radiographic grade in predicting prognosis and urgency of treatment.
  • In practice, any patient with acute pain and severe lameness who cannot walk should be considered unstable until proven otherwise.

items.foco: Radiography: Klein line and Trethowan sign
items.title: Radiography: Klein line and Trethowan sign
items.contentHtml:
Radiography: Klein's line and Trethowan's sign

  • On the anteroposterior radiograph of the hip, the Klein's line is traced along the upper border of the femoral neck; It normally intersects the epiphysis, but in epiphysiolysis it passes outside (Trethowan sign).
  • The positive Trethowan sign is the absence of intersection of Klein's line with the femoral head, indicating epiphyseal displacement.
  • The projection in frog position (Lauenstein) is more sensitive to detect minimal displacements (grade I).
  • X-rays should always be requested. of both hips to compare and detect asymptomatic contralateral involvement.

items.title: Varus displacement and external rotation
items.contentHtml:
Varus displacement and external rotation

  • The typical displacement of the femoral epiphysis is varus and external rotation: the femoral head slides posteriorly and inferiorly with respect to the neck.
  • On the anteroposterior radiograph, this results in a decrease in the head-neck angle and an apparent superimposition of the epiphysis on the neck.
  • External rotation of the limb is the compensatory posture adopted by the femoral head. the patient to relieve capsular tension.
  • Recognition of this deformity is key to differentiating epiphysiolysis from other causes of adolescent lameness such as Perthes disease (which usually presents collapse and fragmentation).

items.foco: Orthopedic emergency: in situ screw fixation
items.title: Orthopedic emergency: in situ screw fixation
items.contentHtml:
Orthopedic emergency: in situ screw fixation

  • Femoral epiphysiolysis is an orthopedic emergency; the treatment of choice is in situ fixation with a cannulated screw (percutaneous single screw).
  • In stable cases, prophylactic fixation is performed without attempting to reduce the epiphysis, as manipulation increases the risk of avascular necrosis.
  • In the unstable form, emergency surgical fixation should be performed (within 24 hours) to minimize vascular damage.
  • In situ fixation stops displacement and allows closure. physeal, with good functional results if the diagnosis is early.

items.foco: Risk of avascular necrosis of the femoral head
items.title: Risk of avascular necrosis
items.contentHtml:
Risk of avascular necrosis of the femoral head

  • Avascular necrosis of the femoral head is the most feared complication, with an incidence of 3-5% in stable forms and up to 50% in unstable forms.
  • The mechanism is the interruption of retinacular blood flow (mainly the medial circumflex femoral artery) due to epiphyseal displacement.
  • The risk It is greater if there is a delay in diagnosis, attempts at forced reduction or late fixation.
  • Avascular necrosis manifests radiologically with collapse of the femoral head and sclerosis, and clinically with persistent pain and joint stiffness.

items.title: Postoperative chondrolysis
items.contentHtml:
Chondrolysis as a postoperative complication

  • Chondrolysis (loss of joint space) is a postoperative complication characterized by the reduction of articular cartilage, with an incidence of 3-7%.
  • It presents as pain, stiffness and loss of mobility of the hip weeks or months after in situ fixation.
  • The exact mechanism is uncertain, but is related to penetration intra-articular screw or with a synovial inflammatory response.
  • Treatment is symptomatic with anti-inflammatories and physiotherapy; In severe cases it may require arthroplasty.

items.foco: Evaluate contralateral hip in all cases
items.title: Contralateral hip evaluation
items.contentHtml:
Evaluate the contralateral hip in all cases

  • Up to 20-40% of patients present bilateral involvement, whether synchronous or metachronous (up to 18 months later).
  • Therefore, it is mandatory to evaluate the contralateral hip clinically and radiologically (anteroposterior and frog position radiography) in all cases.
  • If contralateral hip presents signs of displacement or even radiological suspicion, prophylactic screw fixation is recommended.
  • Contralateral evaluation reduces the risk of a second emergency surgery and associated complications.

items.foco: Differential with transient synovitis and Perthes disease
items.title: Differential diagnosis
items.contentHtml:
Differential with transient synovitis and Perthes disease

  • Transient synovitis of the hip is self-limited, typical in children aged 3-8 years, without obesity, with preserved internal rotation and normal radiography; it resolves in days.
  • Perthes disease affects children aged 4-9 years, with primary avascular necrosis and an x-ray that shows fragmentation and collapse of the femoral head, not epiphyseal displacement.
  • In epiphysiolysis, the patient is an obese adolescent, the x-ray shows varus displacement and Klein's line is abnormal, unlike the other entities.
  • Ultrasound can show joint effusion in synovitis, but is not diagnostic of epiphysiolysis; X-ray is the gold standard.
